exits.label: ◂ back to the causeway (if it's where you left it)

npcs.name: the Wayward Mile
npcs.title: a milestone at a bend that wasn't there a moment ago — the road, throwing something at you
npcs.hail: *(A worn milestone stands where the road bends in a way you are nearly certain it did not, a moment ago. There is no number carved on it — or rather, the number keeps quietly *changing*, counting something. The longer you look, the more the air around it feels *expectant*, like the pause before a stranger speaks, or a card turns, or a thing happens to you that has never happened to anyone in quite this way and never will again. Step close, and the road will show you what it has.)*

variants.text: *(The wayward mile shivers again. The road — restless, fond of you now, or perhaps only bored — turns itself over once more, and something that was not there a moment ago, is.)*

*(A milestone you do not remember passing stands at a bend in the causeway you were fairly sure was straight a breath ago. It is worn smooth by the passage of more souls than there have ever been souls, and as you near it the air *shifts*, the way a held breath shifts before a word. The road clears its throat. Something is about to happen — the particular kind of something that happens to a soul exactly once, and is never twice the same something.)*

nodes.speaker: the Wayward Mile
nodes.text: *(The road turns over.)*

nodes.speaker: the Wayward Mile
nodes.text: *(The milestone has gone still and ordinary, just a worn grey stone at an ordinary bend. The road is only a road. Whatever caprice it held for you, you have spent — the strange visits a soul only so many times in one passage, and you have had your share of its surprises. *(the faintest grind, like a stone settling)* …it will be *different*, the next life. …it always *is.*)*

nodes.speaker: the Wayward Mile
nodes.text: *(A soul sits cross-legged on the milestone's base, flipping a coin that catches a light there is no source for. He is dressed in the rags of a fine coat, and he grins at you with the bottomless, friendly emptiness of a man who has bet everything so many times that betting is the only thing left of him.)* …*there* you are. *(he doesn't ask your name)* …i've a coin, cold one, and a *proposition.* one flip. *call* it. …*win*, and i'll tell you *one* *true* *thing* about the *road* ahead of you — and i *don't* *lie*, it's the *one* virtue a *gambler* keeps. …*lose*, and i keep something *small* of yours. a *memory* you won't *miss* — the *name* of a street you grew up on, the *taste* of a thing your mother made. *(he flips the coin, catches it, covers it)* …*(quietly)* …the coin's *me*, by the way. what's *left.* worn so *thin* you can *see* the *light* *through* me. …*call* it, cold one. …or *don't.* …the *house* respects a *folded* *hand.*

choices.text: *(call it — and let the coin, which is a man, decide)* "Heads. Flip it."
"No bet. I'll keep my memories and you'll keep your edge. The house always wins, and I'd rather not be the house's dinner."

nodes.speaker: the Wayward Mile
nodes.text: *(He uncovers the coin, looks, and his grin goes — not wider, but *gentler.*)* …heads. *(a breath)* …well *called.* …here's your *true* *thing*, cold one, and i'm *sorry* for it, because the *true* ones *always* *cost*: …*you've* *walked* *this* *road* *before.* …not in *metaphor.* …the *bend* you came round to *find* me — you've come round it *other* *times*, in *other* *lives*, and *flipped* this *same* *coin*, and i have told you this *same* *true* *thing* … …and you *forgot*, because *forgetting* is the *toll* the *road* *takes* to let you *walk* it *fresh.* *(he flips himself, idle)* …*win* or *lose*, cold one …the *house* …is the *loop.* …*now* you *know* one *more* *time.* …*spend* it *better* …than you did the *last.*

nodes.speaker: the Wayward Mile
nodes.text: *(He flips, catches, uncovers — and *stops.* The coin has landed on its *edge.* It stands, impossibly, balanced, the light pouring *up* through it. He stares at it like a man seeing a god.)* …*on* its — *(barely)* …*nine* *thousand* *years*, cold one, and i have *never* — it's *never* — *(he laughs, wild, wet)* …*neither.* not *heads*, not *tails.* …the *one* result the *house* has *no* *rule* for. *(he plucks the coin — himself — from its edge and presses it into your hand, and it is *warm*)* …*take* it. *take* *me.* …a soul that can make the *loop's* *own* *coin* land on the *one* *outcome* it *didn't* *plan* *for* …is a soul that might land *itself* somewhere *new.* …*keep* me in your *pocket*, cold one. …and when the road feels *fixed* …*flip* me. …*remember* there's *always* …an *edge.*

nodes.speaker: the Wayward Mile
nodes.text: *(He uncovers the coin, and clicks his tongue, not unkindly.)* …tails. *ah.* …*pay* up, then, cold one — you *called* it, fair's *fair.* *(he reaches out, two fingers, and *takes* something from the air just beside your temple, light as lifting a hair)* …*there.* …a *small* one, like i *promised.* …the — *(he examines it, pockets it)* …the *name* of a *lane.* somewhere you were *small* and *happy*, once. …*(gently)* …don't *grasp* after it; it's *gone*, and grasping only *hurts.* …you'll *feel* the *shape* of where it *was*, some *quiet* *evening*, and *wonder* …and that *wondering*, cold one, is the *only* *interest* the *house* *charges.* …*off* you *go.* …you *played.* …*most* *don't.*

nodes.speaker: the Wayward Mile
nodes.text: *(He flips — but his thin fingers *fumble* it, and the coin — *himself* — spins off the milestone and rolls, edge-on, into a crack in the causeway, gone into the dark below. He *lunges* after it with a sound of pure animal panic, scrabbling at the stone.)* …*no* — *no*, *no*, *catch* it, *catch* — *(it's gone)* …*(he sits back, hollow, staring at the crack)* …that was — cold one, that was *me.* the *thin* part. the *betting* part. …i've *dropped* *myself* into the *underneath.* *(a cracked laugh)* …*don't* — don't go *after* it, you'll *only* *follow* it *down.* …*some* things you *drop*, cold one, you *let* *lie.* …*(he looks at his own hands, faded now)* …*tell* you what. …*that* was the *bet*, after *all*, and the *house* *lost.* …*go* on. …you *owe* me *nothing.* …a man who *drops* his *own* *soul* down a *crack* …has *no* *standing* …to *collect.*

nodes.speaker: the Wayward Mile
nodes.text: *(He tips an invisible hat, the grin never faltering.)* …*smart.* …*smart* one. *(he pockets the coin — himself — with a flourish)* …the *only* *winning* *move*, some say, is *not* to *play.* …*they're* *wrong*, mind — the only winning move is to *play* and *win*, and the *second*-best is to *play* and *lose* and *learn* — but *not*-*playing* is a *respectable* *third*, and *most* souls don't *manage* even *that.* …*(he winks)* …*go* on, cold one. *keep* your *little* *memories.* …*one* of these *lives* …you'll *flip* me. …*they* *all* *do, in* the *end.* …that's the *thing* about a *coin* …*everybody* …*eventually* …*wants* to *know.*

nodes.speaker: the Wayward Mile
nodes.text: *(A dog is sitting in the middle of the road. Not a ghost-dog, not a horror — a *dog*, brown and ordinary and patient, the kind that waited by a door its whole life. It is the Same Dog, the one the stories say hears the Wall's song, though no one knows whose it was. It looks at you, gets up, trots over, and gently sets a small object at your feet: a bone, smooth with carrying, and scratched into it — by teeth, painstakingly — is a *name.* A name you have never heard. The dog sits back, and watches you, and waits to see what kind of soul you are.)*

choices.text: *(crouch, and take the bone, and the name on it)* "…All right. I'll carry it. I don't know whose name this is — but you went to a lot of trouble, and I'll keep it until it means something."
*(follow the dog — it clearly wants to show you something)* "Where, then? Show me. I'll come."
*(kneel and bury the bone gently in the roadside)* "I think some names want rest, not carrying. Let me put this one down somewhere quiet."

nodes.speaker: the Wayward Mile
nodes.text: *(You take the bone. The name is *Elsa.* The dog's whole body changes — a great, silent, full-body wag, relief and gratitude and something almost like *finally* — and it presses its warm head once into your palm, and then it is simply a dog again, trotting off down the road on some errand only it understands.)* *(You don't know who Elsa is. But the bone is warm in your pocket, and you have the strangest, surest feeling that one life — this one, or the next, or the ninth — you will be standing somewhere terrible, and someone will need a name they've lost, and you will have it, because a dog you met on a road that wasn't there decided you were the sort to be trusted with it. *(softly)* …good dog.)*

nodes.speaker: the Wayward Mile
nodes.text: *(The dog leads you off the road, to a low place where the grey goes thin, and sits, and tips its head — *listen.* And you do, and you hear it: faint, far under everything, the Wall's song, the sound of the dissolving. The dog has been coming here for longer than you can guess, carrying bones with names scratched on them to the very edge of the thin place, and setting them down where the song is loudest — *delivering* them, name by name, to the ones being unmade, so that somewhere in the dissolving a soul hears its own name in a dog's careful keeping and knows, for one more moment, that it *was* someone. *(the dog looks at you, and there is no grief in it, only work)* …no one taught it this. No one asked. It simply found a thing that needed doing, in a country full of gods who wouldn't, and it does it, every day, for nothing, because it was the kind of dog that waited by a door — and the dead, it decided, are only souls waiting by a door no one opens. *(you leave it to its rounds. you will think about it for the rest of the game.)*

nodes.speaker: the Wayward Mile
nodes.text: *(You dig a small hollow at the road's edge and lay the bone in it, name-up, and cover it. The dog watches the whole time, grave and attentive, and when you pat the earth flat it comes and sits beside the little grave, and leans its weight against your knee, and the two of you stay there a moment in the quiet. *(then it huffs, satisfied, and trots away)* …you'll never know if you did right — whether *Elsa* wanted carrying or wanted rest. But the dog didn't object, and a dog that hears the Wall's song knows more about what the dead want than you do, and it *stayed*, and *leaned*, which dogs do not do for the wrong choice. *(some names want carrying. some want a quiet place and someone to sit with them a moment. you guessed. the dog forgave you either way — that is the entire and bottomless mercy of dogs.)*

nodes.speaker: the Wayward Mile
nodes.text: *(A lectern stands in the road where no lectern was, and behind it, dabbing his eyes with a black handkerchief, is Corval the Weeping Auctioneer — who, the market says, sells the belongings of the harvested and keeps every name. Tonight he has no goods on the block. He has a *sound.*)* …*ah*, a *bidder*, *bless* you — *(sniff)* …i've a *singular* *lot* tonight, cold one, *singular.* …the *last* *word* a *soul* *ever* *said.* *(he holds up an empty hand, reverent)* …a stranger to you. *died* alone, the *word* *unheard* — and an *unheard* *last* *word*, cold one, is a *terrible* thing, it just … *hangs*, in the *nothing*, *said* to *no* one, *forever.* …i *can't* let that *stand.* *(he meets your eyes)* …i'll not take *coin.* the *price* is *this*: you *carry* the *stranger's* *name*, *out* of here, in your *memory*, so the *word* has *someone* it was *said* *to* at *last.* …*(barely)* …*will* you *bid?* …it's only a *word*, cold one. …and it is the *whole* *of* a *life.*

choices.text: *(bid — take the stranger's name, and hear the word it was waiting for)* "I'll carry it. Tell me the name, Corval, and let me be the one it was said to. Nobody should say their last word to nobody."
"I can't, Corval. I'm carrying too many names already — I'd do this one a disservice, taking it on tired. File it kindly."

nodes.speaker: the Wayward Mile
nodes.text: *(Corval's face crumples with gratitude. He leans across the lectern and tells you the name — *Hettie Mallow*, a seamstress, eighty-one, who died in a chair by a cold window with no one in the house — and then, cupping his hands as if carrying water, he gives you the word she said, to the empty room, with the last breath she had:)* …*(her voice, somehow, through his hands, thin and certain and not afraid)*: …*"…oh, how silly. I've left the kettle on."* *(and that is all. that is the whole of it. not a prayer, not a name, not a regret — a small domestic nothing, an eighty-one-year-old woman's mind reaching, at the very end, for the ordinary task of a Tuesday, because the ordinary was where she had been happy.)* *(Corval is weeping openly, and so, you find, are you.)* …*carry* her, cold one. …*Hettie* *Mallow.* …*she* *left* the *kettle* *on.* …and *now* …*someone* *knows.*

nodes.speaker: the Wayward Mile
nodes.text: *(Corval nods — he has heard *no* before, and he does not judge it, which is somehow worse than if he did.)* …*of* *course.* …a name carried *tired* is a name carried *poorly*, and you're *right* to know your *limit*; most *don't*, and *crack* under the *weight* of *strangers.* *(he dabs his eyes and, with enormous tenderness, writes the unheard word into a great black ledger)* …*back* you go, then, *little* *word.* …*unsold.* …*unheard*, a while *longer.* …*(to you, gently)* …*don't* *grieve* it, cold one. i'll *try* again, *next* *bidder.* …a *word* can *wait.* …that's the *one* *mercy* of the *dead*: …we have *nothing* …*but* …*time.* …*go* *well.* …and if a night comes you've *room* for *one* *more* *name* …*you* *know* where the *lectern* *isn't.*

nodes.speaker: the Wayward Mile
nodes.text: *(A beggar sits against the milestone with a bowl, and there is something *wrong* with the edges of him — they *shimmer*, doubled, as if he can't quite decide how many of him there are. He lifts the bowl.)* …*alms*, kind one? …a *coin*, a *kindness*, *anything* — *(and as he speaks you see it: a faint second beggar, *overlapping* him, mouthing the same words a half-beat behind)* …i was — in *life* i was *generous*, kind one, *gave* and *gave*, *couldn't* *help* it, *every* soul who *asked* … *(the doubling worsens)* …and *now* — *now* every kindness i ever *did* is a *thread* *pulling* me *apart*, and i *can't* *stop* *asking*, and *every* time someone *gives* …*(he winces)* …there's *more* of me, and *less.* …*spare* me *something*, kind one? …*please?* …i'm so — *(both mouths)* …i'm so *thin.*

choices.text: *(read what's actually happening to him before you act)* "Hold still — both of you, all of you. Let me look at what's pulling you apart."
*(give him something — of course you do, he's begging)* "Here. Take it. Whatever I've got."

*(refuse — but with the one truth that might heal him)* "No, friend — and listen to why, because it's the only thing that'll help: you already gave. It already counted. You don't have to keep paying a debt you settled a thousand times in life. Stop asking. You're allowed to be whole."

nodes.speaker: the Wayward Mile
nodes.text: *(You look, and your sense finds the cruel mechanism plain: this is a soul whose *self* was made entirely of giving, who in life poured itself out to every asker and was *beloved* for it — and who arrived in death so *identified* with the act of giving that it cannot conceive of itself *not* giving, and so every act of generosity, here, literally *divides* it, splits it to give a piece away, until it is splitting endlessly, a self that gave itself away so completely there is almost no self left to give. And your sense reads the way *out*, which is the cruel paradox: that the only thing that will *not* divide him further is *refusal* — that to *give* him a coin is to *split* him again, and the one kindness that *heals* is to *decline* it, and tell him the giving already *counted*, that he is *allowed* to *keep* himself. *(you understand now what to do — and that the loving thing and the giving thing are, just this once, opposites.)*

nodes.speaker: the Wayward Mile
nodes.text: *(You give him something — a coin, a kindness, it doesn't matter what — and the instant it leaves your hand he *splits*, cleanly, into *two* beggars, both blinking, both grateful, both lifting their bowls.)* …*bless* you — *(bless you)* — kind one — *(kind one)* — …*(and they see each other, the two of them, and a flicker of dawning horror crosses both faces, because they understand what you just did with your kindness)* …*oh* — …*oh*, *no*, you've — …we were — i was *one*, and now we're — *(they both start to weep, and the weeping doubles the shimmer, and you can see, sickeningly, the *third* and *fourth* of them beginning to bud)* …*don't* — *(both, then four mouths)* …*don't* give us *more*, kind one. …*please.* …your *kindness* …is *killing* us. …*(go gently. you meant well. that is precisely how it spread.)*

nodes.speaker: the Wayward Mile
nodes.text: *(He freezes, bowl half-lifted. The doubled edges *shiver* — and then, slowly, like two reflections sliding back into one pane of glass, the second beggar *settles into* the first, and there is one of him, just one, solid and whole and staring at you.)* …*it* — *(he touches his own chest, his own face, his own single solid self)* …it *counted?* …all of it — the *giving*, in *life* — it — it *counted*, and i don't have to — *(he begins to laugh, a sound like a man setting down a weight he forgot he was holding)* …*nobody* *ever* — they all just *gave* me *coins*, kind one, *thinking* it *kind*, and *every* coin *split* me *thinner* — and *you're* the *first* who *refused* me, the *first* who said *stop*, *you've* *done* *enough*, *keep* *yourself* — *(he stands, whole, light)* …*that's* the *trick* of it, isn't it. …*some* souls …you *save* …by *giving* them *nothing* …*but* …*permission* …to *stop.* …*thank* you, kind one. …you gave me the *one* alms …that *didn't* …*divide* me: …*"enough."*

nodes.speaker: the Wayward Mile
nodes.text: *(A soul in a checked suit and a too-wide smile steps briskly into your path, already talking, a battered case in one hand.)* …*sir* or *madam* — and a *cold* one, *even* *better*, you'll *appreciate* *quality* — have you got a *moment* for the *opportunity* of an *afterlife?* *(he pops the case; inside, nestled in velvet, is a small black *orb* that hurts to look at)* …*behold.* one *apocalypse* — *gently* *used*, *one* *careful* *owner*, *barely* *deployed.* …*end* of the *world*, *complete*, *some* *assembly* *required.* …the *previous* *proprietor* — a *very* *important* *client*, *very* *pale*, *won't* *name* *names* — had it *custom*-*built* and then, *between* us, *lost* his *nerve.* …*(he leans in, conspiratorial)* …*runs* *like* a *dream*, cold one. *kills* *everything.* …i can do you a *very* *fair* *price.* …*interested?*

choices.text: *(see what it actually is, and what he actually is)* "Open the case wider, salesman. Let me see the orb — and let me see you. What is this thing, really, and how did you come to be selling it?"
"Not buying. I don't know what's in that case, but I know a thing dressed up as a bargain when I see one, and the end of the world isn't a curio. Close it up."
*(take it — there are reasons a soul might want the world's ending in its pocket)* "…How much. Quietly. I have my reasons, and they're mine."

nodes.speaker: the Wayward Mile
nodes.text: *(You look, and the patter falls away, and you see the whole sad machine of it. The orb is real — a genuine engine of unmaking, *Myrkul's*, the Pale Companion's, an apocalypse he commissioned in some cold century and then, finding the Unmade's *larger* hunger rising, abandoned as too small. And the salesman — your sense reads it under the checked suit — is a soul Myrkul *used* to build the thing, a clever dead artificer promised meaning and given a task, and then *discarded* when the task was done, left *bound* to the failed product, *compelled* to sell the very device that consumed his afterlife, forever, to anyone who'll stop. He doesn't *want* you to buy it. He *can't* *stop* *offering*. The smile is *welded* on. *(he sees you see, and for one second the patter cracks, and what's underneath is a craftsman's bottomless shame at being remembered only as the salesman of his own undoing.)* …*(don't buy it. but now you know: even the apocalypse, in this country, is just one more discarded soul, holding a thing it hates, hoping someone will finally see the maker and not the wares.)*

nodes.speaker: the Wayward Mile
nodes.text: *(The smile doesn't waver — it *can't* — but something behind the eyes flickers, and for a heartbeat you think it might be *relief.*)* …*no* *sale.* …*quite* *all* *right*, cold one, *quite* — *(he snaps the case shut, almost *gently*)* …*not* for *everyone*, an *apocalypse.* *niche* *product.* *(he straightens his checked lapels, and the patter resumes, but quieter, and there is a terrible thing under the cheer as he turns to go)* …*someone* *buys*, *eventually*, cold one. …*they* *always* *do.* …that's the — *(the smile strains)* …that's the *thing* about an *ending*: …it only *needs* …*one* …*yes.* …and i've *forever* …to *find* it. …*(he tips his hat, and walks on down a road that loops back to you, and you understand he will be here, offering, every passage, until someone says the yes — and you are very, very glad it was not you.)*

nodes.speaker: the Wayward Mile
nodes.text: *(The salesman's smile somehow *widens*, and he does *not* tell you a price — he simply presses the case into your hands, light as a held breath, and steps back with the air of a man whose long, long shift has, against all hope, just *ended.*)* …*sold.* …*(barely, and for the first time the welded smile is a real one)* …*oh*, *thank* you, cold one. …*thank* you. …you've *no* *idea* — i'm — i can *stop* *now.* …*(he is already fading, unbound, released the instant the thing changed hands)* …*mind* it, won't you. …it *runs* …*like* a *dream.* …and cold one — *(fading)* …*you* *bought* it. …which means *you* *decide* …if it *ever* …*runs.* …*that's* the *whole* …*terrible* *gift* of it: …the *end* of the *world* …is *yours* *now* …to *never* *use.* …*(and he is gone, and the case is in your hands, and it is the heaviest light thing you have ever held.)*

nodes.speaker: the Wayward Mile
nodes.text: *(A jester stands frozen in the road, mid-gesture, mid-grin, one finger raised — and then he *moves*, delivering, with impeccable timing, the *setup* of a joke: )* …*so* a *paladin*, a *lich*, and a *tax-collector* walk into a *tavern* at the *end* of the *world*, and the *barkeep* says — *(and he stops. The grin holds. The finger stays raised. He blinks, and resets, and does it again:)* …*so* a *paladin*, a *lich*, and a *tax-collector* — *(you understand: he died, on stage, in the half-second before the punchline, and he has been here ever since, three centuries, *forever* one beat from the laugh, the funniest part of him — the landing — locked behind the breath he never finished. He sees you. His eyes, above the frozen grin, are *desperate.*)* …*(help me,)* they say. *(or: let me be. he cannot tell you which he wants. perhaps you can.)*

choices.text: *(feed him the ending — pick up the joke and land the punchline for him)* "…and the barkeep says — let me, friend. Hand it here. *And the barkeep says: 'We don't serve your kind' — and all three of them say 'which kind?' — because not one of them, paladin nor lich nor taxman, has ever once been told they were welcome anywhere.'* There. There's your landing."

*(leave him the setup — maybe the joy was never the landing)* "…No. I don't think I will, friend. Because I think the punchline would *end* you — and you've spent three centuries in the one place a joke is most alive: the half-second of *almost*, when anything could happen and everyone's still leaning in. I'll let you keep it. Stay in the breath before the laugh. It's the best part. It always was."

nodes.speaker: the Wayward Mile
nodes.text: *(You land it — and your timing is *perfect*, the pause exactly long enough, the turn exactly cruel-then-kind — and the jester's three-centuries-held breath finally, *finally* releases, and he *laughs*, and the laugh is the most astonishing sound, three hundred years of stored-up delight all at once, and the *road* laughs, and the dead for half a mile *laugh* though they can't hear the joke, because a thing that was *stuck* has come *unstuck* and joy like that is *contagious* across any distance.)* …*(he is laughing and dissolving at once, not unmade but *completed*, a soul that needed only its last beat to be *whole* and able, at last, to go)* …*"which* *kind"* — *(he wheezes)* — *oh*, that's — that's *BETTER* than mine, cold one, that's — *(laughing, fading, radiant)* …*three* *hundred* *years* …for a *laugh* …and you *gave* me a *better* *one* …than the *one* i *died* on. …*that's* — *(gone, but the laugh hangs in the air a moment after, like warmth after a fire)* …*that's* the *only* *eulogy* …a *comic* …ever *wanted.*

nodes.speaker: the Wayward Mile
nodes.text: *(You land it — not perfectly, the pause a hair too long, the turn a touch telegraphed — but you land it, and the jester's held breath releases, and he laughs, a real laugh, relieved and grateful, even if it hasn't the three-centuries-thunderclap of a perfect landing.)* …*(he laughs, and loosens, and is able to go)* …*there* it *is.* …*ah.* …*ah*, that's — that's *good*, cold one, that's *kind*, and it's — *(he wipes his eyes, fading gently)* …it's not *quite* how *i'd* have — i'd have *held* the *pause* a *beat* *less*, the *greed's* in the *waiting* — but *who* *cares*, *who* *cares*, it *LANDED*, after *three* *hundred* *years* it *finally* — *(softening, going)* …*thank* you. …a *comic* *dies* *twice*, they say — *once* when the *heart* *stops* …and *once* when the *last* *laugh* *fades.* …you *gave* me the *second* one *proper*, cold one. …*most* …never *get* it …*at* *all.*

nodes.speaker: the Wayward Mile
nodes.text: *(You reach for the landing — and *flub* it. The punchline comes out mangled, the timing off, the turn that should have been cruel-then-kind just *confusing*, and it lands with a soft, terrible *nothing*, the comedian's worst nightmare: the *silence* after a swung-at joke.)* *(The jester's grin doesn't break — he is far too professional for that — but his eyes close, briefly, in the particular pain of a craftsman watching a beautiful setup die a second death.)* …*ohh.* …*no, that's* — *(he winces, gracious through it)* …it's all *right*, cold one, it's — the *timing's* a *cruel* *mistress*, she's *humbled* *better* than *you* — …*(he resets, the finger lifting, the setup beginning again)* …*so* a *paladin*, a *lich*, and a *tax-collector* — *(he is stuck again, another century at least, but he gives you a tiny rueful nod as you go, comedian to audience: *thanks for trying. it's harder than it looks. it was always harder than it looks.*)*

nodes.speaker: the Wayward Mile
nodes.text: *(It goes worse than wrong — you not only flub the punchline, you flub it *confidently*, with a flourish, making it *about you*, the soul who Swept In To Help, and the jester has to stand there, frozen grin and all, and *watch* a stranger bomb his joke and then look *pleased* about it.)* …*(a long, professional silence)* …*welp.* *(the jester's eyes, above the grin, go flat with the ancient weariness of every artist who has watched an amateur mistake enthusiasm for talent)* …*that's* — *yep.* *that's* a *choice* you *made*, cold one. *out* *loud.* in *front* of *people.* …*(he resets, with enormous dignity)* …*tell* you what. …*you* *go* on *ahead.* …leave the *jokes* …to the *dead* *professional* who *died* *doing* them, *eh?* …*so* a *paladin*, a *lich*, and a *tax-collector* — *(and he is stuck again, but you get the distinct impression he'd rather three more centuries of the setup than one more of your help. fair. that's fair.)*

nodes.speaker: the Wayward Mile
nodes.text: *(He looks at you — and the desperation in his eyes, above the frozen grin, slowly changes into something else: understanding, and then a wild, delighted gratitude, because you are the first soul in three hundred years to grasp the thing he had half-forgotten himself.)* *(He gives you, very slightly, a *wink* — the only motion he can make that isn't the joke — and settles, contentedly, *gladly*, back into the held breath, the raised finger, the eternal almost.)* …*(and you understand you have not trapped him; you have *freed* him from the one thing worse than waiting forever: the *ending*. for a comic, the laugh is a little death — the joke, *over*. but the *setup*, the *lean-in*, the *anything-could-happen* — that is the joke *alive*, forever, and he gets to live in it, the best part, the part before the fall.)* *(you leave him mid-almost, three words from a punchline he will now never need, the happiest stuck soul on the whole road — a joke that never has to end, told by a man who finally understands that was the gift all along.)*

variants.text: *(The road turns over — and there is a card on the ground at your feet that was not there, the Tower, face-up, and when you look up Calloway is simply *there*, leaning on the milestone in his impossible wine-red coat as if he'd been waiting an age and enjoyed every second of it.)* …*there* you *are.* *(he beams)* …*small* *world*, the *dead* one — *smaller* than the *living*, and i *own* more of *it.* *(he spins a marker between his fingers — wine-red, his own name on it, the one he owes you)* …i don't *like* *carrying* a *debt*, cold one. it *itches.* and you've a *look* about you tonight — the *look* of a soul whose *road* has just gone *hard.* *(the smile warms)* …*funny* *thing*, that. …*almost* like the *house* …*arranged* …to *be* *here* …*precisely* *now.*

*(The road turns over — and the warm, low laugh reaches you before he does. Calloway steps out of a shadow that has no business holding a man, immaculate, delighted, turning a marker with *your* mark on it between two fingers.)* …*don't* look so *startled.* *(he tuts, fond)* …a *marker's* a *marker*, cold one, and a soul that *owes* *Calloway* is a soul *Calloway* *finds* — it's not *magic*, it's just *very*, *very* good *bookkeeping.* *(he holds up your mark to what passes for light)* …and i find myself, *tonight*, with a *use* for the *favor* you *owe* me. …*unspecified*, you'll *recall.* *my* *call.* …*(the grin sharpens, but there's something almost *gentle* under it)* …*relax.* …it's *nothing* that'll *cost* you a *thing* you'd *miss.* …*quite* …the *opposite.*

*(The road turns over — and Calloway is there, and he is holding *nothing.* No cards. No marker. No deck. From Calloway, an empty hand is the single most disarming thing in the dead lands, and he knows it, and he spreads both of them wide so you can see they're empty, and *grins.*)* …*the* one who *said* *no.* *(he says it like a toast)* …*relax*, cold one — there's *no* *play* here, no *angle*, the *table's* back at the *house* and i *left* it *there.* *(he tips his head, studying you with frank, undisguised fondness)* …i just — *(and for once the patter stalls, and what's underneath is almost shy)* …i *heard* the *road* got *cruel* for you. and i found, to my *considerable* *annoyance*, that i *cared.* …*me.* …*caring.* *(he shudders theatrically)* …*disgusting.* …so i *came.* …*owing* you *nothing*, and *owed* *nothing*, which is — cold one, do you *understand* how *rare* that *is?* …a soul i can *simply* …*talk* to.*

*(The road turns over — and Calloway is leaning on the milestone, perfectly at ease, as though the bend in the causeway were an extension of his own warm establishment.)* …*fancy* *meeting* *you* *here.* *(the grin)* …and *don't* give me the *'how' —* i'm the *house*, cold one, and the *whole* *dead* *world* is *my* *floor*; i turn up *where* the *odds* are *interesting*, and *you*, friend, are the most *interesting* *odds* in *creation.* *(he produces, from nowhere, a single card, face-down, and offers it)* …*now.* …since the *road's* *thrown* us *together* …*shall* we make it *worth* the *coincidence?*

choices.text: *(he owes you — call it in, here, at the knife-edge)* "You picked the perfect moment, Calloway, and we both know it wasn't luck. Yes. I'm calling it in. The favor you owe me — I need it *now*, this road, this door. Pay your debt, Honest Devil."
*(you owe him — face the collection)* "All right. Here it is, then — the favor of your choosing. I signed it; I'll pay it. What does Calloway want?"

*(the one who walked clean — hear why he really came)* "You came all this way, owing me nothing and owed nothing, for the one soul who gave you not a copper. That's not a con, Calloway. That's a man who wanted company. Why me?"
"No card. No coincidence I trust. Tell me straight, Calloway — what are you doing on my road?"

nodes.speaker: Calloway
nodes.text: *(He flicks the wine-red marker into the air, catches it, and *crushes* it in his fist — and the moment it's gone, the hard thing on the road ahead of you simply… *resolves*: the locked way opens, the impossible debt is quietly settled, the door you could not pass swings wide, and you understand he has spent, freely and without flinching, exactly the favor he owed.)* …*there.* *paid.* *in* *full.* *(he dusts his hands, immensely satisfied)* …*nobody* believes me, cold one, when i say it, so *watch* and *learn*: *Calloway* *honors* *every* *debt.* *every* one. it's the *only* law i *never* bend, because a *house* that *welches* is a *house* with *no* *players*, and a *gambler* who *welches* …*(he shrugs)* …is just a *thief* in a *nicer* *coat*, and i've *standards.* *(he looks at the opened way, then at you, and the smile is, for once, plain and warm and without a hook)* …you *beat* me *straight*, that night. *fair* *cut*, *fair* *call.* and *this* — *(he gestures at the road, unbarred)* — *this* is what it *bought* you: the *one* *power* in *all* of *creation* …*reliably*, *unfailingly*, *gladly* …*in* *your* *debt* …and *good* for it. …*(he tips an invisible hat)* …*spend* your *next* *marker* *wiser*, cold one. …*oh* — *wait.* …you *don't* *have* one. …you're *square* with the *house* *now.* …*pity.* …i did *so* …*enjoy* …*owing* you. …*(and he's gone, and the road is open, and it is the most dependable thing that has ever happened to you in the land of the dead.)*

nodes.speaker: Calloway
nodes.text: *(He produces a small sealed token — wine-red wax, no sigil — and presses it into your hand, closing your fingers around it, and for the first time since you met him his voice drops out of the patter entirely, into something low and serious and almost *careful.*)* …*here's* the *favor*, cold one, and it's the *whole* of it: …there's a soul at the *lowest* *table* in my *house* — you'll *know* the *one*, *faded*, *gappy*, *sold* *himself* for *parts.* …*Em.* …*(he doesn't quite meet your eyes)* …you give him *that.* …and you tell him it's *paid.* …*all* of it. *the* *whole* *debt.* *forgiven*, *cleared*, *gone.* …and when he *asks* who — *and* he *will* — …*(his jaw tightens)* …you tell him it was *no* one. a *stranger.* an *anonymous* *benefactor.* the *house's* *charity*, *anything* — …*anything* …but *me.* *(he steps back, and rearranges the smile, but it doesn't quite take)* …*because* if it gets *out*, cold one — if *one* *soul* in the *dead* *lands* *learns* that *Calloway* *forgave* a *debt* …*for* *nothing* …out of some *sentimental* *rot* i *thought* i'd *traded* *away* *centuries* *ago* — …i am *finished.* *ruined.* every *marker* i *hold* …goes *soft.* *(he meets your eyes at last, and there it is, the one crack in the whole gorgeous edifice)* …*that's* my *favor*, cold one. …*spend* my *secret* …*keeping* it. …*launder* my *one* *mercy* …*through* a soul i *trust* …because you're the *only* one who *walked* my *floor* …and saw *both* *halves* of me …*at* *once.* …*go* on. …and cold one — *(barely)* …*never.* …*tell.* …*him.*

nodes.speaker: Calloway
nodes.text: *(He's quiet a moment — genuinely, the most charming soul in the afterlife caught without a line — and then he laughs, softly, at himself.)* …*why* *you.* *(he turns a card he isn't betting, idle)* …because, cold one, i am the *freest* *thing* in this *whole* *grey* *swindle* — i *owe* the *gods* *nothing*, the *Wall* *nothing*, that *grief* under the *stones* *nothing* — and do you *know* the *one* *cost* of being *that* *free?* …*(he says it lightly, and it isn't light at all)* …there's *no* one *at* my *level.* *everyone* *plays* me, or *fears* me, or *wants* a *piece* — *marks*, all of them, *lovely* *marks* — and a *soul* can go a *very* *long* *four* *hundred* *years*, cold one, being the *cleverest* *creature* in *every* *room* …and *never* *once* …have an *equal* *to* *talk* *to.* *(he flicks the card to you — it's a warning, you realize, a name and a place, *real* and *free*, a danger on your road ahead and exactly how to slip it)* …*take* that. *no* *charge.* …a soul that *walked* my *floor* and *folded* the *winning* *hand* …is the *closest* thing to an *equal* i've *had* …since *death.* …and i find i'd *like* you to *keep* *living*, cold one — *selfishly*, *entirely* selfishly, because the *dead* *lands* are *so* *much* *duller* …*without* …*one* *good* *player* …who *isn't* *playing* me. *(the grin returns, gentle)* …*go* on. …*mind* that *danger.* …and *come* by the *house*, *sometime* …*not* to *deal.* …just …to *make* the *odds* *interesting.*

nodes.speaker: Calloway
nodes.text: *(He laughs, delighted to be challenged, and tucks the card away.)* …*straight* it *is.* …*honestly?* — and i'm *always* *honest* — i turned up because the *house* gets *quiet*, cold one, and *you're* the most *entertaining* *account* on my *books*, *open* or *otherwise.* *(he spreads his hands)* …*no* *deal* tonight. *no* *marker.* i just *wanted* to *see* …what the *one* *Returned* who *interests* me …*does* out *here*, on the *road*, with *no* *table* *between* us. *(he steps back toward a shadow that's already taking him)* …*carry* on, cold one. *the* *door's* always *open*, the *first* *drink's* *always* *free* …and the *odds* on *you*, friend — *(the last of the grin, and it's almost *fond*)* …*shorten* …*every* *time* …i *see* you. …*do* *keep* *surprising* me. …it's the *one* thing …i *can't* *buy.*

variants.text: *(The road turns over — and Calloway is there, and he is *diminished.* The wine-red coat is the same, immaculate as ever, but it hangs differently now, on a soul with no house behind it; the magnetic tilt of the world toward him has gone slack. He is, for the first time, simply a man on a road. And when he sees you — the soul who ruined him — he does the single most disarming thing left in his repertoire: he *smiles*, and it is real, and it is *warm*, and it does not have one grain of hate in it.)* …*ah.* …*the* *soul* who *held* the *knife.* *(he spreads his empty hands — emptier than ever now)* …*and* *used* it. …*(he tips his head, studying you, and there is no rancor, only a connoisseur's appreciation)* …*go* *on*, then, cold one. …*say* your *piece.* …i find i *can't* *quite* *hate* *you* for it …and *believe* me …*(the ghost of the old grin)* …i have *tried.*

*(The road turns over — and Calloway is there, leaning on the milestone, but the patter doesn't come, and the cards don't come, and he just *looks* at you for a long, unguarded moment with the still, careful expression of a gambler who has bet everything on a single read and is waiting to learn if he read right.)* …*you* *know* *something*, cold one. *(quietly)* …i *gave* it to you — *handed* it *over*, the *one* *card* that *kills* me, *trusting* a soul i've known *one* *evening* to *carry* my *throat* in their *pocket* and *not* *cut* it. …*(he doesn't smile; this is the realest you've ever seen him)* …i don't — cold one, i've not *slept* in *four* *hundred* *years* and i find i'm *not* *sleeping* *harder*, lately, over *you.* …so. …*here* i *am.* …*owing* you *nothing*, *owed* *nothing*, *holding* *nothing* …except the *knowledge* that you could *end* me with a *sentence* …and *haven't.* …*why?*

choices.text: *(to the ruined Calloway — own it, square)* "I freed a hundred souls you had in chains, Calloway. Em walked out whole. That was worth your house — even worth your fear, which I know is the thing you actually lost. I won't pretend it didn't cost you everything. But you'd have done the same in my seat, and you know it."

*(to the trusting Calloway — tell him why you kept it)* "Because you showed me the one decent thing you were ashamed of, Calloway — that under the whole gorgeous racket, you forgive debts for nothing — and a soul who'd ruin a man for his single act of mercy isn't a soul I want to be. You bet everything that I'd read you right. You did. I kept the knife. Keep your house."
"What happens now, Calloway? For you, I mean. The house, the markers, the four hundred years — what's a magnificent bastard do when the game finally changes on him?"

nodes.speaker: Calloway
nodes.text: *(He listens, and nods, slowly, the way a man nods at a bill he knows is correct.)* …*you're* *right.* …*all* of it. …i *did* keep them in *chains*, and i *did* call it *generosity*, and the *hundred* are *worth* more than the *one* — *(the ghost-grin)* …i *taught* you that *math* *myself*, didn't i. *'a* soul is *worth* whatever it can *charge' —* …and a *hundred* *charge* *more* than *one.* …*(he looks at his own empty hands)* …and *yes*, cold one. it wasn't the *house* i *lost.* houses are *cheap*; i've *built* and *lost* a *dozen.* …it was the *fear.* …*four* *hundred* *years* i was the *one* *thing* in the *dead* *lands* that *nothing* *frightened* …because i *owed* *nothing* and *feared* *nothing* and *every* *soul* *feared* *me* … …and *now* a *bookkeeper* in *grey* has *posted* a *notice* …and i am *just* …*a* *man.* …*(and here is the magnificent thing: he begins, softly, to laugh)* …*do* you *know*, cold one …i think it's the *most* *interesting* thing …to *happen* to me …since *death.* …*(he wipes his eye, genuinely delighted under the loss)* …*four* *hundred* *years* of *winning* …is *boring*, friend. …*nobody* *tells* you that. …and you've just *dealt* me …the *one* *hand* …i *don't* *know* how to *play.* …*(he straightens, and something of the old magnetism flickers back, defiant)* …*watch* me *learn* it. …i'll be *back*, cold one. *smaller*. *for* *now.* …but a soul that *claimed* *himself* once, with *nothing* …*(the grin, returning)* …can *do* it *again.* …*you* didn't *kill* me, friend. …you just …*raised* the *stakes.* …*gods*, i'd *forgotten* …how *good* …*that* *feels.*

nodes.speaker: Calloway
nodes.text: *(For a long moment the most charming soul in the afterlife says nothing at all — and you watch the gambler learn he read the hand right, and watch what that does to a creature who has trusted, fully, exactly once in four hundred years and bet his existence on it. When he speaks, the patter is entirely gone, and what's left is bare.)* …*(barely)* …*huh.* …*(he looks at you like you're a card that came up impossible)* …i *had* a *speech* *ready*, cold one. *several.* one to *buy* your *silence*, one to *threaten* it, one to *charm* it — i've spent *four* *hundred* *years* *learning* to *make* a *soul* do *anything* …and i walked out here with *all* *three* *loaded* …and *not* *one* of them *fits* …a soul that *just* …*didn't* *cut.* *(he exhales, and something in him that has been clenched for centuries, lets go)* …you *understand* what you've *done*, don't you. …you've made me — *(he can hardly say it)* …*safe.* …with *someone.* …for the *first* *time* …since i *decided*, in the *cold*, that *no* one *would* *ever* *hold* a *piece* of me *again* …because *holding* a *piece* of *Calloway* …is how you *end* him. …*(he meets your eyes)* …and you *hold* the *whole* of me, cold one. the *throat*. the *secret*. the *soft* *rotten* *centre* i'd *die* before *admitting* to. …and you've *had* it …and *kept* it …and *asked* me for *nothing.* …*(the grin returns, but it's different now, unguarded)* …*Calloway* …*owes* you. …and *not* a *marker* — *markers* are *cheap.* …i owe you the *one* thing i have *never* *once* *given* *anyone* in *four* *centuries* …*(barely)* …a *friend* who *knows* the *worst* of me …and *stayed.* …*come* by the *house*, cold one. …*any* time. …*you'll* find …the *first* *drink's* *free* …and so is …*everything* *after.* …*just* *you.* …*don't* *tell* a *soul.* …it'd *ruin* me. …*(and for once you both know he means it as a joke, and as the truest thing he's ever said, at once.)*

nodes.speaker: Calloway
nodes.text: *(He considers the question with the relish of a man who genuinely loves a good one.)* …*what* *does* a *magnificent* *bastard* *do* …when the *game* *changes.* *(he grins)* …*the* *same* thing he *did* the *first* time, cold one, when the *Wall* had him and the *gods* had *thrown* him *out* and he had *nothing* but a *cold* *seat* by the *abyss*: …*he* *deals* *again.* …*(he spreads his hands)* …the *game* *changing* isn't the *end*, friend — it's the *only* *part* worth *playing.* a *fixed* *game* is a *dead* one; i've *run* a *fixed* one for *four* *centuries* and i'll tell you a *secret* the *house* *never* *admits*: …it got *boring* about *century* *two.* …*(the old fire, undimmed)* …*you*, cold one — *you* and that *grey* *auditor* and that *grief* under the *stones* and the *whole* *turning*-*over* *world* — you've made the *table* *interesting* *again.* …and an *interesting* *table* …*(he taps the milestone like felt)* …is the *only* *thing* *Calloway* …has *ever* *truly* *wanted* …to *sit* *at.* …*deal* *me* *in*, friend. …*whatever* *comes.* …i've *played* *worse* *hands* …than *'everything's* *changed'* …and *won.* …*(the grin)* …*ask* the *gods.* …they *threw* me *out* …and i *built* an *empire* in their *graveyard.* …*this* is just …*the* *next* *hand.*

nodes.speaker: the Wayward Mile
nodes.text: *(The road turns over into a tidy little office that should not be outdoors, with a counter, a brass bell, and a sign in immaculate lettering: RETURNS & GRIEVANCES — ALL DEATHS FINAL — COMPLAINTS RECEIVED 9 TO 5. Behind it stands the ghost of a shopkeeper, sleeve-gartered, pencil behind one ear, radiating the magnificent unkillable patience of a soul who spent forty years saying 'have you got your receipt.' He brightens at a customer.)* …*afternoon.* …you'll be wanting to *file*, i expect — *everyone* who *finds* this counter wants to *file.* *(he licks a thumb, peels a form off a stack a mile high)* …*Grievances* *against* the *Management*, that's my *department.* the *Management* being — *(he gestures vaguely upward, and also downward, and also at a grey wall on the horizon)* …*all* of it. the *gods.* the *order* of *death.* the *Wall.* *(he sets the form down, smooths it)* …*now.* …i'll not *lie* to you, like *some* would: it goes *nowhere.* there is *no* *higher* *desk.* the *Management* does *not* *read* its *mail.* …*but* — *(he taps the form, and his voice drops into something almost sacred)* …it gets *filed.* *stamped.* *received.* …and a *grievance* *received*, friend …is a *grievance* that *someone*, *somewhere* …*wrote* *down.* …*do* you want to *file?*

choices.text: *(file a formal grievance against the Wall of the Faithless itself)* "Yes. I want to file a complaint against the Wall of the Faithless. For every soul it ever discarded, dissolved, or walled alive for the crime of belonging to no god. Give me the form. I'll fill out every line."
"If it goes nowhere and no one reads it, why do you keep the desk?"

nodes.speaker: the Wayward Mile
nodes.text: *(You fill it out. All of it. The complainant: every godless soul. The grievance: the whole grey atrocity, named, item by item, in a steady hand. And the shopkeeper does not hurry you, does not smirk, does not say it won't matter — he simply waits, with grave professional dignity, and when you slide the finished form across the counter he reads every word, and nods, once, and brings down a heavy brass stamp with the full weight of his arm.)* *RECEIVED.* *(the ink is very red, and very final, and somehow it is the most satisfying sound you have heard since you died.)* …*there.* *(he files it, careful, in a drawer that is somehow full of thousands of others in thousands of hands)* …it's *in* the *system* now, friend. …the *system* that *doesn't* *work*, that *no* one *reads*, that *changes* *nothing.* *(he meets your eyes, and there is iron under the mild manner)* …but you *said* it. *officially.* *on* the *record.* …the *Wall* would *prefer* you *dissolved* *quiet*, *agreeing* you were *nothing.* …and *instead* you stood at a *counter* …and *lodged* a *formal* *objection* …to the *management* of *the* *universe.* …*(he tucks the pencil back behind his ear)* …*most* souls *never* *file*, friend. they just …*endure.* …*you* *filed.* …*come* *again.* …the *desk* …is *always* *open.*

nodes.speaker: the Wayward Mile
nodes.text: *(He considers the question with the seriousness of a man who has had four hundred years to consider it.)* …*why* *keep* the *desk.* *(he straightens a stack)* …because, friend, i was a *shopkeeper* for *forty* *years*, and i learned the *one* *thing* a *counter* *teaches*: …a *person* with a *grievance* and *nowhere* to *bring* it …*curdles.* goes *bitter.* *rots* from the *inside* round the *unsaid* *thing.* …but a *person* who gets to *stand* at a *counter* and *say* *'this* was *wrong*, and i *want* it *on* the *record' —* …*(he taps the bell, lightly)* …*that* *person* …can *go* *home.* …even if *nothing's* *fixed.* *especially* if *nothing's* *fixed.* …the *filing* *is* the *fix*, friend. not of the *grievance.* …of the *griever.* …*(a mild, unkillable smile)* …the *Management* *built* a *universe* with *no* *complaints* *department.* …so i *built* *one.* …it's the *one* *act* of *defiance* …a *shopkeeper* *knows* *how* to *make*: …*excellent* *customer* *service* …in a *cosmos* …that *offers* *none.*

nodes.speaker: the Wayward Mile
nodes.text: *(The road turns over onto a battlefield — two ragged lines of dead soldiers dug in across a strip of grey mud, hurling themselves at each other, falling, reforming, charging again, in a loop you can feel has been running a very long time. A sergeant on the near side hauls himself out of the mud and clutches your sleeve, desperate.)* …*you* — you're not *one* of *ours* and not one of *theirs*, you can *cross* the *line* — *(he gasps)* …*listen.* this *war*, friend — in *life* it lasted *two* *minutes.* *two.* a *skirmish* at a *ford* nobody *remembers*, both *companies* *wiped* in the *time* it takes to *boil* an *egg* — and we've been *fighting* it *here* …*three* *hundred* *years.* *(his voice cracks)* …because *nobody* *won.* nobody *lost.* we just *died*, all at *once*, *mid*-*charge* …and *came* *here* …*still* *charging.* …and we *all* — *both* *sides* — we are *so* *tired*, friend. we *want* to *stop.* *(his jaw sets, miserable)* …but you *can't* *stop* a *war* by *yourself* *stopping* — that's just *losing* — and *nobody* …*nobody* …will be the *first* …to *lay* *down* …*arms.*

choices.text: *(broker it — get both sides to lay down arms in the same instant, so no one is first)* "Then no one will be first. Listen to me — both lines, at once. I'll stand on the ridge between you and count to three, and on three every soul on this field sets down their weapon together. Not a surrender. A simultaneous, mutual, no-one-went-first end to a war that ended three hundred years ago. Will your side hold to a three-count if I swear the other will too?"
"Why does it have to be no one first? You're all dead. You all want to stop. What's left to lose?"

nodes.speaker: the Wayward Mile
nodes.text: *(You climb the ridge between the two exhausted armies, and you do the impossible thing: you make both sides believe, at once, that the other will hold. You count — slow, clear, carrying — and on three, three hundred years of war end in a single, soft, simultaneous sound: the sound of every weapon on a field touching the ground at the same instant, so that not one soldier, on either side, can say they were the one who yielded first.)* *(For a moment nobody moves. Then a man on the far line begins, quietly, to weep, and then the whole field is weeping, both sides, three centuries of held breath let go at once — and they are not enemies now and never really were, just two companies of frightened boys who died at a ford in two minutes and have wanted, every day since, to go home.)* …*(the sergeant grips your arm, undone)* …it's — they're *down.* *both* lines. …*nobody* *broke.* *nobody* *won.* we just — *(he laughs, wet)* …we just *stopped.* …*together.* …*three* *hundred* *years*, friend, and the *only* thing we *needed* …was a *soul* with *no* *side* …to *count* to *three.* …*(soldiers from both companies are walking toward the middle now, hands open)* …*go* *well*, friend. …you didn't *end* a *war.* …a *war* that *size* …*ended* in *two* *minutes*, *long* ago. …you ended the *part* that *hurt*: …the *part* where we *couldn't* …*let* …*each* *other* …*stop.*

nodes.speaker: the Wayward Mile
nodes.text: *(You broker it — not perfectly; a few soldiers on each side hesitate, distrust dies hard after three centuries — but enough hold to the three-count that the line breaks, the charge doesn't reform, and the loop, for the first time in an age, simply… stops. The field goes quiet. It is a ragged, imperfect, real peace.)* …*they're* — most of them are *down*, friend. *(the sergeant breathes)* …not *all.* a *few* on *each* side *couldn't* *trust* it, *still* *clutching* their *blades*, *watching* the *other* line — *old* *fear* *dies* *hard.* …but the *charge* didn't *reform.* and a war that *can't* *charge* …*(he looks around at the stillness)* …*isn't* a war *anymore.* it's just …*tired* *men*, in a *field*, *finally* *allowed* to *sit* *down.* …*thank* you, friend. it's not the *clean* *ending* the *poets* *want* — *(a worn smile)* …but a *three*-*hundred*-*year* *war* …*ending* *messy* …is *still* *ending.* …*go* on. …*we'll* …*manage* the *rest* …*from* *here.*

nodes.speaker: the Wayward Mile
nodes.text: *(You try — you stand on the ridge, you count — but the trust isn't there, not yet; on three, a soul on one line flinches, raises a blade instead of lowering it, and the whole fragile arrangement collapses back into the charge, the loop reasserting itself, three hundred years of reflex swallowing one stranger's count.)* …*(the sergeant sags)* …*close.* …*gods*, that was *close*, friend — i *saw* it, half the line *started* to *lower* — and then *Harla* *flinched*, she *always* *flinches*, and — *(he wipes mud from his face)* …*don't* — don't *blame* yourself. *three* *hundred* *years* of *'never* be *first'* …doesn't *un*-*learn* in *one* *count.* …*(he looks at the reforming lines, weary past despair)* …*come* *back*, if the *road* *brings* you. …*try* *again.* …*every* *count* that *gets* *half* the *line* to *lower* …is *half* a *line* …that *remembers* …it *wanted* to. …*next* *time* …*maybe* …the *other* *half* …*remembers* *too.*

nodes.speaker: the Wayward Mile
nodes.text: *(It goes wrong fast: you climb the ridge and call for the surrender too plainly — 'lay down your arms, the war is over, you've lost' — and the word lands like a spark in dry grass, because to three hundred years of soldiers who never yielded, 'you've lost' is the one thing worse than fighting forever, and BOTH lines turn their fury, briefly and unanimously, on the outsider who dared call them beaten.)* …*down!* *get* *DOWN* — *(the sergeant yanks you off the ridge as something whistles past)* …*gods*, friend, you *can't* — you *can't* say *'surrender'* to *these* *men*, you can't say *'lost' —* …*(he hauls you to cover)* …that's the *one* *word* …*three* *hundred* *years* of *dying* …*taught* them to *hate* *more* …than *each* *other.* …*(he catches his breath)* …it's *not* a *surrender* they *need*, friend. *nobody* *surrenders.* it's a *way* to *stop* …that *isn't* *losing.* …*'no* one *first.'* *'both* at *once.'* *'a* *draw.'* …*those* they might *hear.* …*'you've* *lost' —* …*(he shakes his head)* …go. …*come* *back* …when you've *thought* of a *way* …to *let* a *soldier* …*stop* …*without* …*kneeling.*

nodes.speaker: the Wayward Mile
nodes.text: *(The sergeant laughs, bitter and tired, scrubbing mud from his eyes.)* …*'what's* *left* to *lose.'* *(he shakes his head)* …you're *thinking* like a *living* *soul*, friend — like it's about the *ford*, the *ground*, the *winning.* …it was *never* about the *ground.* *(quietly)* …we were *boys.* *both* sides. *scared* *boys* who'd *never* *killed* anyone, *charging* a *ford* because *someone* *told* us to, and we *died* …all of us …in the *time* it takes to *change* your *mind* …*before* we *could.* …and the *one* thing, friend, the *one* thing a *boy* who *died* *scared* and *charging* *clings* to …is that he didn't *run.* didn't *yield.* *stood.* …it's *all* we *have.* …*all* we *brought* *across.* …and to *lay* *down* *first* …is to *say* …*'i* was the one who *broke' —* …and a *boy* who *broke* …*(his voice cracks)* …has *nothing* *left* …in the *whole* *grey* *country* …to *call* his *own.* …*that's* …*what's* *left* to *lose*, friend. …the *last* *thing.* …*not* the *war.* …the *not*-*having*-*run.*

nodes.speaker: the Wayward Mile
nodes.text: *(The road turns over into an orchard, and it takes you a long, vertiginous moment to understand what grows here. The trees are bare and grey, and from their branches, instead of fruit, hang hands. Pairs of them, cupped, open, reaching — thousands, gently swaying, every size and age, a baby's and a crone's and a soldier's and a mother's. A soul moves among them with a watering can, tending them, and looks up at you with the serene melancholy of a gardener who loves a strange and sorrowful crop.)* …*mind* the *low* *branches.* *(a soft voice)* …they're *tender*, the *new* ones. …you're *wondering* what they *are.* *everyone* does. *(she touches a pair, fond)* …they're the *hands*, friend. …*every* *hand* …that *ever* *let* *go* …of a *dying* one. *(she moves down the row)* …the *daughter* who *held* her *father's* hand till the *breathing* *stopped* and then …had to …*open* her *fingers.* the *nurse.* the *stranger* at the *roadside.* the *mother* — *(she pauses at a small pair, and her voice goes very gentle)* …*every* *letting*-*go*, friend, *every* *'i* *have* to *let* you *go* *now' —* …it *grows*, *here.* …because a *letting*-*go* like *that* …is *too* *large* …for *one* *soul* to *carry* *alone.* …so the *orchard* *carries* it. …*holds* the *hands* …that *had* to *open.* …*(she offers you the watering can)* …*walk* a *while*, if you *like.* …*see* if you *find* …a *pair* …you *know.*

choices.text: *(walk the rows — look for a pair of hands you know: your own letting-go, or a stranger's you might honor)* "I'll walk. And I'll water the ones no one comes for. If I find a pair I know... I'd like to hold them a moment. Even a stranger's. Especially a stranger's."
"Doesn't it break you, tending this? An orchard of every worst goodbye there ever was?"

nodes.speaker: the Wayward Mile
nodes.text: *(You take the can and walk the grey rows, and you water the hands no one comes for, and somewhere deep in the orchard you stop — because there is a pair you know. Maybe they're yours: the exact shape of your own fingers, caught forever in the half-open curl of the worst morning of a life you're only beginning to remember. Maybe they're a stranger's, but you know them anyway, because a letting-go is a letting-go and you have done it or had it done to you and the shape is the same in every soul. You cup them in your own. They are warm. They have been waiting, it turns out, not to be re-closed — that letting-go was right, was love, was the last gift — but simply to be held by someone who understood that opening them was the hardest thing their owner ever did.)* *(The gardener watches from a distance, and does not intrude, because this is the whole purpose of her orchard: not to undo a single letting-go, but to make sure that no hand that ever had to open over a dying love is left, forever, holding nothing.)* …*(you stay a while. you water a few more. then you set the can down, gently, and go — lighter, and heavier, and more whole than when you came.)*

nodes.speaker: the Wayward Mile
nodes.text: *(The gardener smiles, and it is sad, and it is not broken.)* …*break* me. *(she shakes her head slowly)* …*no*, friend. …the *opposite.* …i *was* *breaking*, you see — *out* *there* — *(she nods at the grey beyond the orchard)* …i was a *soul* who'd had to *let* *go* of *too* *many*, in *life*, a *nurse*, *forty* *years* of *opening* my *fingers* over the *dying* …and i came *here* *certain* …that *every* *letting*-*go* was a *small* *betrayal*, a *failure*, a *hand* that *should* have *held* *on.* *(she touches a pair, tender)* …and then i *found* the *orchard.* …and i *understood.* …*holding* *on* …is *not* the *love*, friend. *anyone* can *clutch.* …the *love* …is the *opening.* …the *terrible*, *generous*, *necessary* *opening* …that *says* *'go* *gently*, i *won't* *make* you *stay* …for *me.'* …*(she resumes her watering)* …so *no.* it doesn't *break* me. …*tending* it *healed* me. …*every* *hand* *here* …did the *bravest* *thing* a *soul* can *do.* …i just …*make* *sure* …they're not *alone* …*for* *having* done it.

nodes.speaker: the Wayward Mile
nodes.text: *(The road turns over and a memory hits you like a wave — vivid, total, and not yours. A kitchen with yellow curtains. The smell of bread and a particular rain. A wedding where you wept, a hand you held for sixty years, a specific blue door, a child's laugh you would know anywhere — an entire life, lived and loved and not for one second your own, flooding into you with the unbearable clarity of someone else's whole heart. And standing in the road before you is a soul with a hole in it — a soul gone strangely blank, vague, unfinished, looking at you with desperate recognition.)* …*you* — *(the blank soul reaches out)* …you have it. i can *see* it on you — the *yellow* *curtains*, the *blue* *door* — that's — *(its voice shakes)* …that's *mine.* that's my — the *Wall* *mis*-*filed* it, friend, when i came *through*, it *takes* the *names* and the *lives* and it *sorts* them and *sometimes* …*sometimes* it *drops* one …into the *wrong* *soul.* …and *you* *caught* *it.* my — *(it presses where a face should be)* …*sixty* *years.* my *whole* — i can't *remember* my *own* *wedding*, friend, because *you're* *remembering* it *for* me, and it's the *best* thing i ever — *(barely)* …*will* you …*give* it *back?* …i *know* it's *beautiful.* …i *know* …what i'm *asking* you to *let* *go* of. …but it's …*mine.*

choices.text: *(give it back — open your hands and return the stranger's whole life, even though it's the most beautiful thing you've held since you died)* "Of course it's yours. Here — take it back. All of it: the yellow curtains, the blue door, the sixty years, the rain. It was never mine to keep, however much I'll miss it. A soul should die remembering its own wedding. Take it."

*(ask the harder thing — whether a memory can be shared instead of surrendered)* "Before I give it back — does it have to be all-or-nothing? Could I hold a copy? Carry your wedding with me, so two souls remember it instead of one? Or does returning it mean losing it from me entirely?"

nodes.speaker: the Wayward Mile
nodes.text: *(You open your hands — your mind — and you let it go, all of it, the kitchen and the rain and the sixty years, pouring out of you and into the blank soul before you, and you feel it leave the way you'd feel warmth leave a room, and then it is gone and you cannot quite recall the color of the door, only that it mattered, only that it was beautiful, only that it wasn't yours.)* *(And the soul before you fills. You watch it happen — the blankness resolving into a face, a whole face, lit from within by a returned life, sixty years rushing home all at once — and it gasps, and laughs, and weeps, and remembers, finally, its own wedding, its own blue door, its own beloved, restored entire to the one soul who had the right to them.)* …*(it can hardly speak)* …*i* — *oh.* …*oh*, *there* she *is.* …*there's* her — i can *see* her *face* again, friend, the — *(it presses both hands to its restored face, overcome)* …you *gave* it *back.* …you *held* the *best* thing i *ever* *was* …and you *handed* it *home* …and *kept* …*nothing.* …*(it looks at you, whole)* …i'll *remember* *you*, now — *you're* in it, the *kind* *stranger* who *carried* my *whole* *heart* a *little* *way* and *gave* it *back.* …*you're* *part* of the *memory* now, friend. …*that's* …the *only* *thanks* …a *soul* has: …*you* *get* …to be *remembered* …*inside* …the *thing* …you *returned.*

nodes.speaker: the Wayward Mile
nodes.text: *(The blank soul goes still, and then — slowly — something like wonder moves through it.)* …*share* it. *(it turns the idea over)* …i don't — friend, i don't *know.* *no* one ever *asked.* every soul who *catches* a *mis*-*filed* *life* either *clutches* it, *keeps* it, *won't* *give* it *back* — *(a flicker of old grief)* …or *hands* it *over* *whole* and *forgets* it *clean.* …*nobody's* ever *asked* …if a *memory* could be *two* *souls'* at *once.* …*(it considers, and a fragile hope rises)* …maybe — maybe it *can*, friend. maybe that's the — maybe that's the *one* *thing* the *Wall* *never* *counted* on: that a *life*, *shared*, isn't *halved.* …it's *doubled.* *two* souls *remembering* the *yellow* *curtains* …isn't *half* a *wedding* *each.* it's the *wedding*, *witnessed* *twice.* …*(it reaches out, and you don't surrender the memory — you let it copy, let it bloom in the blank soul while staying, fainter but real, in you)* …*there* — *oh*, *there* it — *we* *both* — *(it laughs, amazed)* …*i* *remember* my *wedding* …and *so* do *you* …and *neither* of us …*has* *less.* …*(it meets your eyes, transformed)* …*carry* it *gently*, friend. …you're a *witness* *now*, to a *love* you *never* *lived.* …and a *love* with *two* *who* *remember* it …*(barely)* …is a love …the *Wall* …*can't* …*dissolve.*
